message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Optimising statistical power in early-stage trials and observational studies accelerates discovery and improves the reliability of results. Ideally, intermediate outcomes should be continuously distributed and lie on the causal pathway between an intervention and a definitive outcome such as mortality. In order to optimise power for an intermediate outcome in the RECOVERY trial, we devised and evaluated a modification to a simple, pragmatic measure of oxygenation function - the
                  <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>a</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>O</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  /
                  <jats:italic>F</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>O</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  (S/F) ratio.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  We demonstrate that, because of the ceiling effect in oxyhaemoglobin saturation,
                  <jats:italic>S/F</jats:italic>
                  ceases to reflect pulmonary oxygenation function at high values of
                  <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>a</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>O</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  . Using synthetic and real data, we found that the correlation of
                  <jats:italic>S/F</jats:italic>
                  with a gold standard (
                  <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>a</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>O</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  /
                  <jats:italic>F</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>O</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  ,
                  <jats:italic>P/F</jats:italic>
                  ratio) improved substantially when measurements with
                  <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>a</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  <jats:italic>O</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  ≥ 0.94 are excluded (Spearman
                  <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  , synthetic data:
                  <jats:italic>S/F</jats:italic>
                  : 0.31;
                  <jats:italic>S/F</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>94</jats:sub>
                  : 0.85). We refer to this measure as
                  <jats:italic>S/F</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>94</jats:sub>
                  .
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  In order to test the underlying assumptions and validity of
                  <jats:italic>S/F</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>94</jats:sub>
                  as a predictor of a definitive outcome (mortality), we collected an observational dataset including over 39,000 hospitalised patients with COVID-19 in the ISARIC4C study. We first demonstrated that
                  <jats:italic>S/F</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>94</jats:sub>
                  is predictive of mortality in COVID-19. We then compared the sample sizes required for trials using different outcome measures (
                  <jats:italic>S/F</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>94</jats:sub>
                  , the WHO ordinal scale, sustained improvement at day 28 and mortality at day 28) ensuring comparable effect sizes. The smallest sample size was needed when
                  <jats:italic>S/F</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>94</jats:sub>
                  on day 5 was used as an outcome measure.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  To facilitate future study design, we provide an online user interface to quantify real-world power for a range of outcomes and inclusion criteria, using a synthetic dataset retaining the population-level clinical associations in real data accrued in ISARIC4C
                  <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="uri" xlink:href="https://isaric4c.net/endpoints">https://isaric4c.net/endpoints</jats:ext-link>
                  .
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  We demonstrated that
                  <jats:italic>S/F</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>94</jats:sub>
                  is superior to
                  <jats:italic>S/F</jats:italic>
                  as a measure of pulmonary oxygenation function and is an effective intermediate outcome measure in COVID-19. It is a simple and non-invasive measurement, representative of disease severity and provides greater statistical power to detect treatment differences than other intermediate endpoints.
                </jats:p>
title: Evaluation of S / F 94 as a proxy for COVID-19 severity
